visual_checks: Look for any images that contain text (screenshots, banners, headings rendered as images, button images with text)
Check if the text within images could instead be rendered as styled HTML/CSS text
Identify images of text that are purely decorative or serve as visual ornamentation
Look for essential images of text where the specific visual presentation is critical (e.g., a logo or brand wordmark)
Check for text baked into infographics, charts, or diagrams that could be rendered as accessible text
Verify whether images of text can be visually customized by the user (font, size, color)
pass_conditions: No images of text are used, except where the image is purely decorative or the specific presentation is essential (e.g., logotypes)
All informational text is rendered as actual HTML/CSS text rather than as images
Where images of text are essential, text alternatives convey the same information
Logotypes and brand wordmarks are the only instances of images of text on the page
fail_conditions: Text is rendered as images for headings, buttons, or banners when HTML/CSS text could achieve the same presentation
Navigation items or menu labels use images of text instead of styled HTML text
Promotional banners or hero sections contain text as images without an essential visual reason
Images of text are used for body content, paragraphs, or descriptions
Charts or infographics contain text that is not available as accessible text
na_conditions: Page contains no images of text at all
All images of text on the page are logotypes or brand wordmarks where the presentation is essential
ne_conditions: AI response could not be parsed
Screenshots could not be captured for visual verification
Images failed to load so their text content cannot be evaluated
common_mistakes: Using images for stylized headings when CSS web fonts could achieve the same appearance
Navigation menus built with image-based text labels instead of styled HTML links
Banner images with promotional text baked in rather than overlaid as HTML text
Assuming that providing alt text on images of text satisfies this criterion (it does not; the text should be real text)
Using images of text for call-to-action buttons instead of styled HTML button elements
Infographic text that could be represented as an accessible HTML table or list with CSS styling

examples.pass: All headings, navigation labels, and button text are rendered as HTML text styled with CSS web fonts; the only image of text on the page is the company logo
examples.fail: The page hero section, navigation menu, and section headings all use images of text instead of HTML text, even though the same visual style could be achieved with CSS
examples.partial: Most text is rendered as HTML but the page includes several promotional banners with text baked into images that could be rendered as styled HTML overlays

plain_meaning: A stricter version of 1.4.5: images of text are allowed only for pure decoration or when a specific visual presentation is essential to the meaning. Logotypes remain exempt, but general style preferences are not an excuse.
scope_applies_to: Any informational text rendered as an image
Banners, hero text, and marketing graphics containing text
Button and navigation labels rendered as images
Graphics containing quotes or announcements as baked-in text
scope_does_not_apply_to: Images where the particular visual presentation is essential (font specimen, historical manuscript, signature)
Purely decorative images with no text meaning
Elements removed from the accessibility tree (aria-hidden, display:none)
Real text rendered via web fonts or SVG <text> (not images of text)
Captions and other media text (different SCs)
borderline_calibration: Level AAA; apply only during AAA evaluation.
SVG <text> and web-font-rendered text are real text, not images of text.
No exception exists for preferred-font reasoning under AAA.
When the image is decorative with no informational text, do not flag.
